title: Barszcz biały
subtitle: Polish white borscht with smoked sausage and egg
excerpt: A pale, sour soup made with chicken stock, smoked sausage, hard-boiled eggs, and a soured-cream liaison for body. The white counterpart to red borscht and a centrepiece of the Polish Easter table.
sourceNotes: Barszcz biały (white borscht) is traditionally made with zakwas — a fermented rye flour and water starter that provides characteristic sourness. This version uses white wine vinegar and soured cream in its place, which is how many Polish families prepare it outside of the Easter season when zakwas is not already fermenting on the shelf. The smoked sausage here substitutes for the traditional biała kiełbasa (white pork sausage), which is the authentic centrepiece of the Easter version.

recipe.freezeNotes: Do not freeze with the eggs or cream. The stock base can be frozen separately; reheat and add fresh eggs and soured cream when serving.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Keep the base separate from the eggs and soured cream until serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook the sausage and hard-boil the eggs up to a day ahead. Make the stock base and reheat gently; add the cream and eggs just before serving.

content.text: Barszcz biały is the soup Polish families eat at Easter alongside żurek, bigos, and cold cuts. Where red borscht is vivid and sweet, white borscht is pale yellow, sour, and savoury, with a body that comes from the soured cream stirred in at the end. The dominant flavours are marjoram, smoked meat, and a clean vinegar sourness.

The soured cream must go in off the heat or very gently — boiling the soup after adding it will cause it to split and curdle. Stir a spoonful of hot stock into the cream first to temper it, then pour it back into the pot.

Bring the {{stock-chicken}} to a simmer in a large saucepan. Add the {{allspice}}, {{bay-leaves}}, {{garlic}}, {{marjoram}}, and {{smoked-pork-sausage}}. Simmer for 20 minutes. Hard-boil the {{eggs}} in a separate pan for 10 minutes, then peel and halve them.

Remove the bay leaves and allspice. In a small bowl, whisk the {{plain-flour}} into the {{soured-cream}} until smooth, then whisk in a ladleful of the hot stock to temper it. Pour the cream mixture back into the soup and stir well. Add the {{white-wine-vinegar}} a little at a time, tasting as you go — the soup should be clearly sour but not harsh. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Do not boil after the cream has gone in. Ladle into bowls and top each with two egg halves.

items.symptom: Cream has curdled in the soup
items.cause: Cream added to a boiling soup without tempering
items.fix: Always temper by whisking a ladleful of hot stock into the cream first, then add to the pot off the heat. If it has already curdled, blend a portion of the soup and stir it back in — this will partially smooth it out

items.cause: Not enough vinegar, or stock was too rich
items.fix: Add white wine vinegar a little at a time and taste after each addition. The sourness should be present and clean, not sharp

items.cause: Flour not fully incorporated or too much stock
items.fix: Mix a teaspoon of plain flour with a little cold water to a paste, whisk it into the simmering soup, and cook for 2 minutes

content.text: White borscht is Easter food in Poland: it appears on the table alongside red borscht, żurek, kielbasa, and painted eggs. Outside the holiday, it is a home soup rather than a restaurant dish — simple, restorative, and best understood in relation to the red version it quietly mirrors. Both share the same sour character and the same supporting role on a table laden with other things. On its own, it is a complete and satisfying lunch.
